Nested Ternaries — When to Avoid Them
Nesting ternaries is possible but quickly becomes unreadable. One level of nesting is sometimes acceptable; more than one is almost always a sign to refactor:
Ternaries are particularly useful for toggling CSS class names or style properties inline:
You can use ternaries to conditionally pass different prop values or even omit a prop:
When the ternary branches are longer JSX elements, formatting matters for readability. Common conventions:
When the condition itself is complex, extract it to a named variable before the return. This separates the "what to render" from "which condition decides it":

Putting JSX that should not re-render inside the ternary
— when the ternary switches between branches, the discarded branch unmounts and the new one mounts. If you need to preserve state between branches, use
Forgetting to wrap multi-line branches in parentheses
— JSX that spans multiple lines after
calls to conditionally vary cell content, icons, or styles per item.

Each column applies its own independent condition. Keeping conditions inline is fine when each expression is short (two words or a single element). Extract to a helper if any branch grows beyond one element.
Two common ways to hide content are a ternary that mounts/unmounts the element and a CSS class that hides it visually. They are not equivalent.
Content is expensive to keep; state should reset
Animated hide/show; state must survive toggle
Prefer the ternary (unmount) approach unless you have a clear reason to preserve state across hides — unmounting keeps the component tree clean and avoids stale state bugs.
component is a canonical real-world use of ternaries — toggle between a truncated preview and full content.
Three ternaries work together here: one controls which body to render, one controls the button label, and
uses a default parameter (not a ternary) to keep the prop optional. The
attribute receives a boolean directly — no ternary needed there.

when you want to render something or nothing — but only when the left side is a boolean or always positive number.
Do not nest ternaries more than one level. Extract conditions to named variables or use if/else before the return.
Extract complex conditions to named variables before the return for clarity.
